items.q: Was the wartime Grand Alliance based on trust and friendship?
items.correct: No - it was a marriage of convenience against Hitler; once Germany was close to defeat, old suspicions over Poland, Germany and ideology resurfaced at Yalta and Potsdam.
items.misconception: The USA and USSR were close friends who only fell out suddenly after 1945.
items.random_wrong: The Grand Alliance was a formal union of the American and Soviet governments.
items.why: Students assume allies must be friends; understanding the alliance as temporary and pragmatic explains why the Cold War followed so quickly.

items.q: How was the Cuban Missile Crisis actually resolved?
items.correct: Khrushchev publicly withdrew the missiles in return for a US pledge not to invade Cuba, while Kennedy secretly agreed to remove US missiles from Turkey.
items.misconception: Kennedy simply forced Khrushchev to back down without giving anything in return.
items.random_wrong: The United Nations sent peacekeepers to occupy Cuba.
items.why: The secret Turkey deal was hidden at the time, so the 'Kennedy won' story stuck; examiners reward knowing both the public and secret terms.

items.q: Did detente mean the USA and USSR became allies?
items.correct: No - detente was a relaxation of tension through agreements like SALT 1, while rivalry, the arms race and proxy conflicts continued.
items.misconception: Detente meant the Cold War was over and the superpowers were now allies.
items.random_wrong: Detente was a military pact directed against China.
items.why: The word 'thaw' misleads students into thinking hostility ended; detente managed competition rather than ending it.

items.q: What was agreed at the Tehran Conference in 1943?
items.correct: Britain and the USA would open a second front in Western Europe
items.random_wrong: The USSR would join the Marshall Plan
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Britain and the USA would open a second front in Western Europe. “Germany would be permanently reunified” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the purpose of the Marshall Plan?
items.correct: To provide US economic aid to rebuild Europe and reduce the appeal of communism
items.misconception: To create a military alliance against the USSR
items.why: Correct answer: To provide US economic aid to rebuild Europe and reduce the appeal of communism. “To create a military alliance against the USSR” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How did the Western powers respond to the Berlin Blockade?
items.correct: They flew supplies into West Berlin in a massive airlift
items.misconception: They blockaded Soviet ports in return
items.random_wrong: They handed West Berlin to East Germany
items.why: Correct answer: They flew supplies into West Berlin in a massive airlift. “They blockaded Soviet ports in return” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What did the SALT 1 agreement of 1972 do?
items.correct: Limited anti-ballistic missile systems and froze the number of strategic missile launchers
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Limited anti-ballistic missile systems and froze the number of strategic missile launchers. “Banned all nuclear weapons” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did the Cuban Missile Crisis occur?
items.why: The real figure is 1962 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
